title: What Is Haystack? Production LLM Pipelines
metaDescription: Haystack is deepset's production LLM framework built on modular pipelines. Learn how its component-and-pipeline model powers reliable RAG and search apps.
oneLiner: You will understand what Haystack is, how its modular component-and-pipeline architecture works, and why teams pick it for production-grade RAG and search.

blocks.md: **Haystack** is an open-source framework from the company **deepset** for building LLM applications — search, question answering, and especially [RAG](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/what-is-rag) — out of small, swappable parts. Instead of writing one long script that embeds a query, calls a vector database, stuffs the results into a prompt, and calls a model, you assemble those steps as named *components* and wire them together into a *pipeline*.

Think of a kitchen with a row of stations: one station washes the vegetables, the next chops them, the next cooks, the next plates. Each station does one job and hands its output to the next. Haystack is that kitchen for LLM apps. A *retriever* fetches relevant documents, a *ranker* reorders them, a *prompt builder* assembles the text, a *generator* calls the model. You can replace the oven without rebuilding the whole kitchen, and the food keeps flowing along the same line.

So Haystack is two ideas in one: a **library of ready-made components** (retrievers, rankers, prompt builders, generators, document stores, converters), and a **way to connect them** into a runnable graph. You describe *what* should happen and in *what order*; Haystack runs the wiring.

Haystack predates the current RAG wave — it began as an open-source question-answering framework and grew into a general LLM-orchestration framework. That history is why its core abstraction is a *search/QA pipeline*, not a chatbot.

Getting a RAG demo working in a notebook is easy. Getting it to run reliably in production — where inputs are messy, models get swapped, and someone has to debug it at 2am — is the hard part. Haystack is built for that second job. Its whole design is about making an LLM app *structured, inspectable, and replaceable* instead of a tangle of glue code.

items: **Composability.** Each step is a component with a clear input and output. Want to swap your embedding-based retriever for a keyword one, or add a reranker between retrieval and generation? You change one node, not the whole script.

**Provider independence.** Components for many model providers, embedders, and vector stores share the same interfaces. Moving from one model or database to another is mostly a config change, not a rewrite — so you avoid hard lock-in.

**Production concerns built in.** Pipelines are serializable to YAML, can be served behind an API, and expose hooks for logging and tracing. That makes an app reviewable and deployable, not just runnable on your laptop.

**A clear mental model.** Because everything is a component-in-a-graph, a new teammate can read the pipeline definition and see exactly what happens to a query, in order. The structure *is* the documentation.

blocks.md: Who reaches for it? Teams building search and RAG systems they intend to *operate*, not just prototype — internal knowledge assistants, documentation search, support tools, and QA over large document sets. If you want a tidy, swappable architecture more than the absolute fastest path to a one-off script, Haystack fits. It sits in the same space as other [orchestration frameworks](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/build-your-first-rag-app); its distinctive bet is the explicit pipeline graph.
Haystack has three core building blocks: **components**, **pipelines**, and a **document store**. Understand those three and you understand the framework.

A component is a small unit with typed inputs and outputs that does exactly one thing. Common ones include a *converter* (turn a PDF or web page into text), an *embedder* (text to vectors), a *retriever* (find relevant documents in a store), a *ranker* or *reranker* (reorder candidates), a *prompt builder* (assemble the final prompt from a template), and a *generator* (call the LLM). Because every component declares what it consumes and produces, Haystack can check that two components are compatible *before* you ever run the pipeline.

blocks.text: Pipelines: a graph, not a straight line

blocks.md: A pipeline is a graph of components connected by named edges. You add components, then connect one component's output to another's input. The pipeline figures out the run order from those connections. It does not have to be a single straight line — a pipeline can branch (send a query to two retrievers) and merge (join the results before ranking), which is how hybrid search and routing are expressed.

diagram.title: A typical RAG pipeline in Haystack

blocks.text: The document store: where knowledge lives

blocks.md: Retrievers don't hold data themselves — they read from a **document store**, Haystack's abstraction over a place to keep documents and their embeddings (an in-memory store for experiments, or a real vector database for production). The same retriever interface works across stores, so you can develop against an in-memory store and switch to a hosted database later by changing configuration.

Most RAG apps have two pipelines that share one store. An **indexing pipeline** runs ahead of time: convert files, split them into passages, embed each one, and write them to the store. A **query pipeline** runs per question: embed the question, retrieve the closest passages, build the prompt, and generate the answer. Same store, two flows.

Here is the shape of a minimal RAG query pipeline. The exact class names vary by version, so read this for the *pattern* — create components, add them, connect outputs to inputs, then run — rather than as copy-paste code.

blocks.title: rag_pipeline.py — the pattern
blocks.code:
from haystack import Pipeline
# (component classes are imported from haystack and its integrations)

# 1) Build the components — each does one job.
query_embedder = TextEmbedder()        # question -> vector
retriever      = Retriever(store)      # vector -> top-k documents
prompt_builder = PromptBuilder(        # documents + question -> prompt
    template=(
        "Answer using ONLY the context.\n"
        "Context:\n{{ documents | join(' ') }}\n\n"
        "Question: {{ question }}"
    )
)
generator      = Generator(model="...")  # prompt -> answer

# 2) Assemble the graph.
rag = Pipeline()
rag.add_component("embedder", query_embedder)
rag.add_component("retriever", retriever)
rag.add_component("prompt", prompt_builder)
rag.add_component("llm", generator)

# 3) Wire outputs -> inputs. This is the whole structure.
rag.connect("embedder.embedding", "retriever.query_embedding")
rag.connect("retriever.documents", "prompt.documents")
rag.connect("prompt.prompt", "llm.prompt")

# 4) Run. The pipeline computes the order from the connections.
result = rag.run({
    "embedder": {"text": "What is the refund window?"},
    "prompt":   {"question": "What is the refund window?"},
})
print(result["llm"]["replies"][0])

blocks.md: Notice what's *not* there: no manual ordering of steps, no `if` branches to decide what runs next. The `connect` calls describe the graph, and the pipeline derives execution order from the edges. To add a reranker, you'd insert one component between `retriever` and `prompt` and re-point two connections — the rest is untouched.

A pipeline can be saved to and loaded from YAML. That means your app's architecture lives in a config file you can review, version-control, and load at deploy time — handy for keeping prompts and wiring out of application code.

sections.title: Components vs pipelines: the two-layer model

blocks.md: The single most useful thing to internalize is the split between the two layers. Components are the *vocabulary*; pipelines are the *sentences* you build from them.

rows: Does a single job (retrieve, rank, generate)
Coordinates many jobs end to end
Routes one component's output to another's input
Yes — replace one without touching others
Yes — edit nodes/edges, keep the rest
Often — this is your app's structure

blocks.md: Because the contract is *typed inputs and outputs*, you can also write your own component when nothing built-in fits — a custom cleaner, a domain-specific filter, a call to an internal service. As long as it declares its sockets, it drops into a pipeline like any official component. That extensibility is why Haystack scales from a toy QA demo to a real system without changing frameworks.
Most trouble with Haystack is the same trouble as any RAG system, plus a few framework-specific snags.

items: **Mismatched embedders.** The model that embeds your documents at indexing time must match the one that embeds the query at run time. Mix them up and similarity scores become meaningless. Use the same embedding model on both sides.

**Forgetting the indexing pipeline.** A query pipeline retrieves nothing if the store is empty. The indexing run is a separate, earlier step — easy to skip when you're focused on the question-answering side.

**Connecting incompatible sockets.** If component A's output type doesn't match component B's expected input, the connection fails. Read the error: it usually names the two sockets and their types, which points straight at the fix.

**Retrieval quality, not the framework.** When answers are wrong, the cause is almost always *what got retrieved*, not Haystack. Chunking, the embedding model, and `top_k` decide your ceiling — see [why RAG hallucinates](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/why-rag-hallucinates).

**Over-engineering early.** You don't need a branching, reranked, multi-retriever pipeline on day one. Start with embedder → retriever → prompt → generator, confirm it works, then add stages only when evaluation shows you need them.

blocks.md: Documents you retrieve are untrusted input. A page or file pulled into the prompt can carry hidden instructions aimed at the model. Treat retrieved text as data, fence it clearly in your prompt template, and never let it silently become commands.
Once the basic component-and-pipeline model clicks, the interesting parts of Haystack are about handling real-world complexity without losing the tidy structure.

**Branching, routing, and loops.** Because a pipeline is a graph rather than a straight line, you can route a query down different paths based on its content, run two retrievers in parallel for hybrid search and merge their results, or add a step that loops — for example, a validator that sends bad output back to be regenerated. This is how Haystack expresses logic that a simple chain can't.

**Tools and agentic behavior.** Beyond fixed retrieve-then-generate flows, components can give the model tools to call, letting it decide whether and what to retrieve. That turns a static pipeline into something closer to an [agentic](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/what-is-rag) loop driven by the model's own judgment, while still living inside the same pipeline structure.

**Deployment and serving.** A pipeline can be exposed behind an HTTP API and run as a service, with the YAML definition as the deployable artifact. deepset also builds commercial tooling on top of open-source Haystack for hosting, monitoring, and collaborating on pipelines — useful to know when you weigh the open framework against a managed platform.

**Evaluation.** The same composability that builds the app helps you test it. You can assemble an evaluation pipeline that measures whether retrieval surfaces the right documents and whether answers stay faithful to them. As with any RAG system, *measuring* retrieval and faithfulness — not eyeballing a few answers — is what separates a demo from something you can trust in production.

**Where to go next.** If you're choosing an approach, compare [RAG against fine-tuning](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/rag-vs-fine-tuning) and [against long context](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/rag-vs-long-context) before committing. If you're choosing a framework, build the same small RAG app in a couple of them and keep the one whose mental model you can debug fastest. Haystack's bet is that an explicit pipeline graph is the one you'll still understand months later.

faq.a: Haystack is an open-source framework for building LLM applications such as search, question answering, and RAG. You assemble retrievers, rankers, prompt builders, and generators into a pipeline, which makes it well suited to production systems you intend to operate and maintain, not just prototype.

faq.q: Who makes Haystack and is it open source?
faq.a: Haystack is built by deepset and is open source. deepset also offers commercial tooling on top of the open framework for hosting, monitoring, and collaborating on pipelines, but the core framework is free to use and self-host.

faq.q: What is the difference between a component and a pipeline in Haystack?
faq.a: A component does one job with typed inputs and outputs — for example a retriever or a generator. A pipeline is a graph that connects many components and routes each one's output to the next's input, deriving the run order from those connections. Components are the vocabulary; the pipeline is the structure of your app.

faq.q: Can Haystack do more than RAG?
faq.a: Yes. Haystack started as a question-answering and search framework, and its pipeline model handles document search, classification, summarization, routing, tool-using agents, and evaluation — RAG is the most common use, not the only one.

faq.q: How is Haystack different from other LLM frameworks?
faq.a: Its defining feature is the explicit pipeline graph: every step is a component with declared inputs and outputs, wired into a graph that can branch, merge, and loop. That composability and the serializable YAML definition are aimed at making production apps inspectable and swappable rather than just quick to prototype.

faq.q: Do I need a vector database to use Haystack?
faq.a: Not to start. Haystack has an in-memory document store for experiments. For production you swap in a real vector database through the same document-store interface, which usually means a configuration change rather than a rewrite of your pipeline.
